chapters.title: What is Linux?
chapters.content:
Linux is a free, open-source operating system kernel created by Linus Torvalds in 1991. Unlike Windows or macOS, Linux is built on the philosophy that software should be free and modifiable by anyone.

Today Linux powers everything from Android phones to supercomputers, web servers, and yes — Arch Linux desktops like this one 🌸

chapters.title: The Filesystem
chapters.content:
Linux uses a single-root filesystem tree starting at /

/          root of everything
/home      your personal files (~)
/etc       system config files
/bin       essential programs
/usr       user programs
/var       logs, databases
/tmp       temporary files
/dev       hardware devices
/proc      running processes
/mnt       mounted drives

Everything in Linux is a file — even hardware devices.

chapters.title: Essential Commands
chapters.content:
Navigation:
  pwd          print current directory
  ls           list files
  ls -la       list with hidden files + details
  cd folder    enter a directory
  cd ..        go up one level
  cd ~         go home

Files:
  touch file   create empty file
  mkdir dir    create directory
  cp a b       copy a to b
  mv a b       move or rename
  rm file      delete file
  rm -rf dir   delete directory (careful!)
  cat file     print file contents
  less file    scroll through file

Search:
  grep 'text' file   find text in file
  find . -name '*.txt'   find files by name
  which program      find where a program is

chapters.title: Permissions
chapters.content:
Every file has permissions for: owner, group, others

Example:  -rwxr-xr--
  - = file (d = directory)
  rwx = owner can read, write, execute
  r-x = group can read, execute
  r-- = others can only read

Changing permissions:
  chmod 755 file     rwxr-xr-x
  chmod 644 file     rw-r--r--
  chmod +x file      add execute for all
  chown user file    change owner

Common numbers:
  7 = rwx   6 = rw-   5 = r-x   4 = r--

chapters.title: Package Management
chapters.content:
Different distros use different package managers:

Arch Linux (pacman):
  sudo pacman -S package      install
  sudo pacman -R package      remove
  sudo pacman -Syu            update everything
  pacman -Ss keyword          search

Ubuntu/Debian (apt):
  sudo apt install package
  sudo apt remove package
  sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade

Fedora (dnf):
  sudo dnf install package
  sudo dnf remove package
  sudo dnf upgrade

AUR (Arch User Repository) — use yay:
  yay -S package

chapters.title: Processes & System
chapters.content:
Viewing processes:
  top          live process monitor
  htop         nicer version of top
  ps aux       list all processes
  pgrep name   find process by name

Controlling processes:
  kill PID         send kill signal
  kill -9 PID      force kill
  pkill name       kill by name
  Ctrl+C           interrupt running program
  Ctrl+Z           pause program
  bg               resume in background
  fg               bring back to foreground
  command &        run in background

System info:
  uname -a         kernel version
  df -h            disk usage
  free -h          memory usage
  uptime           how long system running

chapters.title: Shell Scripting
chapters.content:
A shell script is a file of commands run in sequence.

Create a script:
  #!/bin/bash          <- shebang line, always first
  echo 'Hello, world!'

Make it executable:
  chmod +x myscript.sh
  ./myscript.sh

Variables:
  name='Alice'
  echo "Hello $name"

If statement:
  if [ $x -gt 5 ]; then
    echo 'big'
  else
    echo 'small'
  fi

For loop:
  for i in 1 2 3; do
    echo $i
  done

While loop:
  while [ $n -lt 10 ]; do
    n=$((n+1))
  done

chapters.title: Arch Linux Specifics
chapters.content:
Arch Linux is a rolling-release distro — instead of version upgrades, you get continuous updates.

Arch philosophy:
  • Simplicity — no bloat, you build it yourself
  • Minimalism — only what you need
  • User-centrism — you are in control

Key Arch tools:
  pacman          package manager
  yay / paru      AUR helpers
  systemd         init system
  pacstrap        install base system
  arch-chroot     chroot into system

Useful Arch commands:
  systemctl start service      start a service
  systemctl enable service     start on boot
  systemctl status service     check status
  journalctl -xe               view system logs
  lsblk                        list drives
  ip addr                      network info

The Arch Wiki is the best Linux documentation on the internet. When in doubt — wiki it.
